---
title: "Knotroot Bristlegrass (Setaria parviflora)"
slug: "setaria-parviflora"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/621878187/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Knotroot Bristlegrass"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Poales"
tax_family: "Poaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Poales", "Poaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/621878187/medium.jpg" alt="Knotroot Bristlegrass" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/acdcarroll">
acdcarroll</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/341589823">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Poales
-   **Family:** Poaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=79068)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Rare species in Florida found in fields, roadsides, marsh edges, and disturbed areas.

## Natural history notes

A perennial grass characterized by its upright stems and cylindrical, bristly seed heads. In Florida, it is commonly found in a variety of habitats including fields, roadsides, marsh edges, and disturbed areas. It grows during the warmer months and spreads by both seed and rhizomes, often forming clumps in open habitats.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/79068-Setaria-parviflora)
-   [Local profile](https://fsus.ncbg.unc.edu/show-taxon-detail.php?taxonid=2327)
